coredata.dc:title: Effect of charging protocols on electrochemical performance and failure mechanism of commercial level Ni-rich NMC811 thick electrode
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Phattharasupakun, Nutthaphon
Bunyanidhi, Panyawee
Chiochan, Poramane
Chanlek, Narong
Sawangphruk, Montree
coredata.dc:description: In order to bridge the gap between academia and industry in the development of cathode active material for high-energy lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), several experimental factors reflecting commercial use need to be considered. Herein, based on a thick commercial level NMC811 electrode, we simply demonstrated the effect of charging protocols on the rate performance, cycling stability, and Li storage mechanism. A constant current charge/discharge, typically used in most publications, was found to exhibit worse rate capability and cycling stability compared to the constant current constant voltage (CCCV) mode of charge, used in commercial LIB cells due to the less uniform Li concentration gradient between the surface and bulk of the material. In operando XRD also revealed that there was no negative impact from the rapid change in lattice parameters towards cycling performance. We also found that the major degradation mechanism of Ni-rich NMC811 showing poor cycling performance across literature, can be mainly ascribed to the thick cathode electrolyte interphase (CEI) resistive layer, hindering Li intercalation at high rate and also lead to lower accessible capacity in each cycle. This work illustrates a simple example of the gaps between research in academic and industry that need to be narrowed for the development of practical high energy materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Li-ion battery
Ni-rich
Charging
CCCV

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrode preparation, electrochemical evaluation, and in operando XRD measurement

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Charge-discharge profiles at different discharge rates and their corresponding dQ/dV vs. V at C/20 of cells using different modes of charge:(a) a constant current charge at different rates, (b) a fixed C/20 charge, and (c) CCCV charge at different rates with C/20 cutoff. All cells were measured at a controlled temperature of 30 °C. Solid and dash lines represent data for duplicate cells.

(a) Rate capability, (b) Cycling test at C/5, and (c-d) Cycling test and their corresponding voltage polarization at 1C of cells using different modes of charge. All cells were measured at a controlled temperature of 30 °C. Fill and empty circles represent data for duplicate cells.

In situ XRD results: Contour plots showing three 2Theta regions of XRD and their corresponding charge–discharge profiles of NMC811 tested with two different modes of charge: (a) a constant current C/20 charge:C/20 discharge and (b) a CCCV C/5 charge:C/20 discharge.
Ex situ XPS results: C1s, F1s, and O1s spectra of NMC811 electrodes after 1C cycling at (a) 1st, (b) 50th, (c) 100th, and (d) 200th cycle.

head.ce:title: Effect of charging protocols on electrochemical performance and failure mechanism of commercial level Ni-rich NMC811 thick electrode

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering
School of Energy Science and Engineering
Vidyasirimedhi Institute of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to bridge the gap between academia and industry in the development of cathode active material for high-energy lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), several experimental factors reflecting commercial use need to be considered. Herein, based on a thick commercial level NMC811 electrode, we simply demonstrated the effect of charging protocols on the rate performance, cycling stability, and Li storage mechanism. A constant current charge/discharge, typically used in most publications, was found to exhibit worse rate capability and cycling stability compared to the constant current constant voltage (CCCV) mode of charge, used in commercial LIB cells due to the less uniform Li concentration gradient between the surface and bulk of the material. In operando XRD also revealed that there was no negative impact from the rapid change in lattice parameters towards cycling performance. We also found that the major degradation mechanism of Ni-rich NMC811 showing poor cycling performance across literature, can be mainly ascribed to the thick cathode electrolyte interphase (CEI) resistive layer, hindering Li intercalation at high rate and also lead to lower accessible capacity in each cycle. This work illustrates a simple example of the gaps between research in academic and industry that need to be narrowed for the development of practical high energy materials.

ce:section.ce:para: With the coming of advanced energy applications like electric vehicles and grid storage, Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) providing high energy density, good rate capability, and long cycling life are needed.[1–3] A tremendous effort from researcher in academia has been made mostly on the development of cathode active materials, starting from LiCoO2, dated back 30 years ago, to LiMn2O4 and LiFePO4, for high-power cells, and eventually to high-energy Ni-containing materials, for example, LiNi1-x-yCoxAlyO2 (NCA) and LiNi1-x-yCoxMnyO2 (NMC).[3,4] However, substantial gaps between academia and industry remain as there are several different experimental factors, for example, thin electrode loading (<10 mg cm−2), excess electrolyte, and different testing protocols have always been used in research papers.[5,6] This could lead to a slow advancement and adoption of practical high-energy LIBs. As a result, most reported active materials with novel modifications and improvement strategies, showing impressive and excellent results have rarely been scaled up or used practically.[7].

Herein, we aim to simply illustrate the difference in testing protocols used to evaluate the performances of LIBs on the commercial level LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2 (NMC811) thick electrode, active loading ∼ 20 mg cm−2, as they can significantly influence battery’s behaviour. Most literature always evaluate their battery performance by reporting capacities measured by a constant current mode of charge and discharge. Even if the cells are to be discharged at high C-rates, the exact same high C-rates will also be used for charge step. This may not be an issue when a very thin electrode is used, but it does not represent the actual rate capability and cycling performance of the material when commercial level thick electrode is used. Generally, a high charging rate provides a quick charge, but also affects battery performance, whilst a low charging rate provides better capacity utilization, but time-consuming, which is inconvenient for EV applications. As for the commercial cell in industry, a CCCV mode of charge has always been used to ensure full Li-delithiation from cathode no matter how much the discharge rates are.[8,9] The use of high rates during the first CC step followed by a slower CV step could circumvent overvoltage and prolong battery life.[10] Note that CCCV technique has also been used across literature, but mostly to activate electrode or enhance Li kinetics. The impact on electrochemical performances is not studied.[11–14] These two different charging protocols could lead to a different result when one evaluates the active material electrochemical performance. We found that these two methods yielded different electrochemical performances, especially at high C-rates in which the CCCV mode of charge provided much better rate capability compared to a typical constant current charge/discharge, that most literature uses.[15,16] In operando XRD also confirms that a faster lattice parameter/cell volume changes did not influence the Li storage properties of the materials. The constant voltage step at top of charge will create uniform Li distribution from surface towards the bulk.[17–19] Interestingly, a capacity retention during cycling is also higher with CCCV, possibly due to the smaller concentration gradient between surface and bulk of the material during the voltage hold. The smaller concentration gradient could lead to a better Li kinetics and more accessible capacity during the subsequence discharge as there are more Li vacancies in the bulk. This is in contrast to the constant current charge–discharge at high rate where Li intercalation takes place mostly on the surface. The CCCV could generate less kinetic limitations and less amounts of Li-containing resistive cathode electrolyte interphase (CEI) layer that hinders Li diffusion as demonstrated by ex situ XPS. It was also found that during long-term cycling at 1C, at a specific point, nearly no capacity can be obtained. However, the check-up capacities, after every 100 cycles, at a low rate of C/20 remained almost the same as before cycling. The results guide us that it could be the CEI layer, generated from interactions between highly delithiated cathode and electrolyte, that limits the performance of Ni-rich materials rather than microcrack formation, phase transformation, or metal dissolution, which have been previously reported as the major degradation mechanism when one sees the poor cycling performance.[20,21] This paper raises an important question to the community in evaluating the performance of LIBs and their compatibility towards industrial scale and also on the degradation mechanism of Ni-rich materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrode preparation, electrochemical evaluation, and in operando XRD measurement
ce:section.ce:para: The electrode disks with a diameter of 12.7 mm were prepared by cutting from a one side-coated NMC811 18650-electrode sheet. The electrode contained 95.2% active material 2.4% carbon black and 2.4% PVDF binder having a thickness of 70 µm coated on an aluminium foil (13 µm).[22] The active mass loading was ca. 20 mg cm−2 which is in the same level as commercial electrode. Electrochemical evaluation was done in half coin-cell configuration (CR2032) using Li metal with a diameter of 14 mm (∼18 mg) as a counter and a reference electrode. 80 µl of 1.2 M LiPF6 in FEC:DMC (1:4) was used as the electrolyte with one layer Celgard PE separator (25 µm). The rate capability and cycling stability of the cells were tested between 3.0 and 4.3 V vs. Li/Li+ using three different charging modes including (i) a constant current charge using different C-rates, (ii) a fixed C/20 constant current charge, and (iii) a CCCV charge using different C-rates with a current cutoff of C/20. (C = 200 mA g−1) Note that a constant current discharge at different rates was used for all modes of charge. Cells were tested using Neware battery tester with a controlled temperature box at 30 °C.

In operando XRD measurement was carried out in a commercial Amiens LRCS cell using NMC811 thick electrodes. Two experiments were performed including (i) a constant C/20 charge with a constant C/20 discharge and (ii) CCCV C/5 charge to C/20 cutoff with a constant C/20 discharge. XRD patterns were continuously measured every 10 min at a 2theta between 10 and 70° using D8 advanced diffractometer (Cu Kα 1.54056 Å,) connected with Autolab potentiostat (PGSTAT 302 N). The Rietveld refinement was done using TOPAS 5.0 software.

The electrochemical performances of NMC811 half coin-cells made from thick electrode (active loading of 20 mg cm−2) were evaluated with three different charging protocols including (i) a constant current charge using different C-rates, (ii) a fixed C/20 constant current charge, and (iii) a CCCV charge using different C-rates with a current cutoff of C/20. All cells were tested in the same following order: 2 cycles at C/20, rate capability test at C/10 to 1.5C every 3 cycles, 2 cycles at C/20, cycling test at C/5, and finally 2 cycles at C/20 (see Fig. 2a-b). The purpose of C/20 charge–discharge at the beginning, after rate, and after cycling test is to ensure whether there is a material degradation or not. Fig. 1
shows the charge–discharge profiles from three different modes of charge (shown in each column) and a constant current discharge at different rates. The first two C/20 cycles of all cells exhibited similar voltage profiles and discharge capacities of ∼ 200 mAh g−1 with a 1st cycle irreversible capacity of 14%. This is similar to other previous works using thinner electrodes as there is no kinetic limit at this very slow rate.[23,24] As the rate increased up to 1C (green line), a large overpotential and large polarization can be observed. Importantly, the charge and discharge capacities using a constant current mode (Fig. 1a1), a typical mode used in most literature, were lowest. Whilst, a fixed C/20 charge and CCCV methods exhibited the exact same capacities at all rates, but the CCCV mode is much faster. The voltage hold at the top of charge (4.3 V) during constant voltage allows Li distribution from the surface of active material into bulk compensating the small Li storage during constant current at high rate.[17,25] This raises an important question on the use of constant current charge and discharge to evaluate material performance whether it represents actual material utilization and actual rate capability or not. To demonstrate that the voltage hold at the top of charge does not have a negative effect on the cycling stability, especially if the active material is Ni-rich, where the degree of parasitic reactions from Ni4+ and electrolyte becomes stronger at higher state of charge or where more Li ions are deintercalated from the structure, the dQ/dV vs. V curves were analyzed as shown in Fig. 1a2-1c2. The dQ/dV vs. V curves at C/20 at 1st cycle, before cycling (after rate measurement), and after cycling showed the typical characteristic of NMC811 material and remained the same indicating that there was a negligible material loss or degradation after multiple Li intercalation/deintercalation from low rates to high rates and even after cycling test with voltage hold for 50 cycles.[26] It is important to note here that even the Li diffusion during delithiation is faster than lithiation,[25] a full charge capacity at high rate can still not be achieved with the use of constant voltage hold.

Fig. 2a-b show the rate capability measurement and cycling test at C/5 using different modes of charge. The capacities at low rates were similar, whilst the capacities at high rates were significantly different between different modes of charge. With the constant current charge at high rates, Li concentration on the surface of NMC811 will reach a concentration threshold of Lix→1TMO2 before the low cutoff voltage of 3.0 V even though the bulk structure can take more Li.[25] This results in a limited capacity when a high constant current was used, and it does not represent the actual rate capability of the material. Fig. 2c compares the cycling stability test at a higher rate of 1C between constant current charge and CCCV charge modes. The discharge capacity from CCCV mode was higher with a better capacity retention up to 75 cycles and the difference is more obvious than the C/5 cycling test shown previously. Eventually, the capacity rapidly dropped to almost zero similar to the case of constant current charge mode, but slower. Previous works have mainly attributed this phenomenon as active material degradation from microcracks, TM dissolution, phase transition, etc.[20,21,24,27] However, the check-up C/20 capacity after 1C cycling for 100 and 200 cycles remained almost the same. This confirms that there was no active material loss or degradation even though no more capacity can be achieved at high rate, at least when the cell was tested up to 4.3 V vs Li. We believe that this is all about the CEI layer with large charge transfer resistance that hinders Li insertion to be accumulated on the outer surface whereby limiting accessible capacity. Whilst, at the low rate of C/20 check-up cycle, accumulated Li on the surface has sufficient time to redistribute into the bulk. Fig. 2d shows the corresponding voltage polarization (ΔV), calculated from the difference between average charge voltage and average discharge voltage, between two modes of charge which is a representative of internal resistance of the cell during cycling. All cells exhibited a gradual increase in ΔV during the first 50 cycles following by a rapid increase in ΔV, corresponding to the rapid drop in capacity. As a result, the CEI layer keeps growing over cycling and eventually it becomes too thick and resistive, hindering Li+ intercalation/deintercalation. Note that the ΔV of all C/20 cycles remained significantly small. The results agreed well with recent literature ascribing rapid capacity fade to mainly the kinetic reason from resistive CEI growth at the interface between active material and electrolyte as particle morphology and Ni content in the Li layer remained almost the same after cycling.[24,28,29] As a result, modification of cathode surface chemistry, for example by coating or using electrolyte additives, could be the key to prolong battery cycle life of those high energy Ni-rich active materials.[30].

To further understand the Li storage mechanism from different modes of charge, in operando XRD was carried out as shown in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3a shows the results where a constant current of C/20 was used, whilst Fig. 3b shows the results where a CCCV C/5 charge was used following by C/20 discharge. Three 2Theta regions corresponding to the reflection planes of (003), (101), and (104) were selected and shown. The NMC811 testing with a slow constant current charge exhibited a typical continuous peak shift with solid-solution behaviour.[22,24] At the initial charging process, the (003) peak, representing the c-axis composing of the TMO6 and LiO6 layers, shifted towards lower 2θ due to lattice expansion from repulsion between TM interslabs following by a rapid shift towards higher 2θ as critical amounts of Li were deintercalated from the structure leading to a rapid decrease in electrostatic repulsion between TM layers. Whilst, the (101) and (104) peaks, representing a-axis, shift towards higher 2θ due to the lattice contraction from oxidation of Ni2+/3+ and Co3+ ions into smaller size Ni4+ and Co4+. During discharge, all the peak shifts similarly but in the opposite direction. In case of the CCCV C/5 charge (Fig. 3b), peak separation can be observed during the initial charge due to a large overpotential and energy barrier of Li deintercalation.[22,31] At the top of charge during constant voltage step, Li distribution from surface to bulk occurred, reaching concentration equilibrium, whereby all peaks shifted to the same point as a slow constant charge process confirming the similar degree of Li storage. Note, the overall charge process is much faster, ca. 5 h compared to ca. 20 h of the typical constant current charge. Fig. 3c confirms that the lattice parameter, a and c, between two modes of charge changed to the same degree, especially at the end of charge. Correlating this finding with previous cycling data, it can be concluded that the rapid change in lattice parameter, which is believed to cause accumulated lattice strain and microcracks, does not affect electrochemical performance of the material. Running cells at high rate all the way to the top of charge following by a voltage hold are much faster and can account for the large overpotential, reflecting the actual rate performance of the material.

To demonstrate the surface layer formation and growth on NMC811, ex situ XPS measurement was carried out on NMC811 electrode after 1C cycling at 1st, 50th, 100th, and 200th cycle as shown in Fig. 4 . From the C1s spectra, the increasing C-O and C = O species over cycles indicates the presence of more oligomer (polyethylene oxide, ROR) and carbonate (ROCO2Li, polycarbonate) compounds, respectively due to electrolyte decomposition.[32,33] This can also be observed from the F1s spectra where the peak ratio between LiPF6/Li x PF y O z
decreased overtime due to LiPF6 salt degradation.[33] The LiF formed from the reactions between LiPF6 and carbonates (RCO3Li) in CEI also increased after the 1st cycle. For the O1s spectra, the lattice O from the NMC811 can be observed only at the 1st cycle indicating that the CEI was thin and possibly not uniform.[32] As the cycle number increased, the lattice O peak disappeared with the reduction of O-surface, CO3, and O-C = O related species due to the organic species and Li2CO3 decomposition. Overall, the content of all these decomposition products in the CEI was found to be higher over cycles, thickening the surface film and became more resistive to Li ions.[33,34] This result explains the reduction in capacities and ΔV increase during 1C cycling which is believed to be the main failure mechanism of NMC811, at least with the testing conditions and electrolyte used in this work.

By comparing two modes of charge, a constant current vs. a constant current constant voltage, on the commercial level thick NMC811 electrode, the rate capability and cycling performance were found to be significantly different. The CCCV mode of charge, typically used by industry but not academia, provides better rate and cycling performances. Voltage hold during CV step at the top of charge could suppress overvoltage and create uniform Li ion distribution from the surface to bulk leading to a lesser degree of kinetic limitations. As shown by in operando XRD, the rapid change in lattice parameters from CCCV does not negatively influence cycling performance of NMC811. In addition, the poor cycling performance at high rate but similar check-up capacities at low rate with no loss also hints us that the degradation mechanism of NMC811 mainly comes from CEI formation and growth.

Nutthaphon Phattharasupakun: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Panyawee Bunyanidhi: Investigation. Poramane Chiochan: Investigation. Narong Chanlek: Investigation. Montree Sawangphruk: Methodology, Validation, Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially support by Thailand Research Fund or renamed Thailand Science Research and Innovation (TSRI) and Vidyasirimedhi Institute of Science and Technology (RSA6180031) as well as the Energy Policy and Planning Office (EPPO), Ministry of Energy, Thailand. The authors would like to thank the Synchrotron Light Research Institute (Thailand) for XPS beamtime.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Issues and challenges facing rechargeable lithium batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.M. Tarascon, M. Armand, Issues and challenges facing rechargeable lithium batteries, Nature, 414 (2001) 359-367.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Li-ion battery materials: present and future

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Nitta, F. Wu, J.T. Lee, G. Yushin, Li-ion battery materials: present and future, Mater. Today, 18 (2015) 252-264.
M. Li, J. Lu, Z. Chen, K. Amine, 30 Years of Lithium-Ion Batteries, Adv. Mater., 30 (2018) 1800561.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A reflection on lithium-ion battery cathode chemistry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Manthiram, A reflection on lithium-ion battery cathode chemistry, Nat. Commun., 11 (2020) 1550.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bridging the academic and industrial metrics for next-generation practical batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Cao, M. Li, J. Lu, J. Liu, K. Amine, Bridging the academic and industrial metrics for next-generation practical batteries, Nat. Nanotechnol., 14 (2019) 200-207.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Critical Parameters for Evaluating Coin Cells and Pouch Cells of Rechargeable Li-Metal Batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.D. Radin, S. Hy, M. Sina, C. Fang, H. Liu, J. Vinckeviciute, M. Zhang, M.S. Whittingham, Y.S. Meng, A. Van der Ven, Narrowing the Gap between Theoretical and Practical Capacities in Li-Ion Layered Oxide Cathode Materials, Adv. Energy Mater., 7 (2017) 1602888.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: State of health estimation of lithium-ion batteries based on the constant voltage charging curve

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wang, S. Zeng, J. Guo, T. Qin, State of health estimation of lithium-ion batteries based on the constant voltage charging curve, Energy, 167 (2019) 661-669.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The effect of the charging protocol on the cycle life of a Li-ion battery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.S. Zhang, The effect of the charging protocol on the cycle life of a Li-ion battery, J. Power Sources, 161 (2006) 1385-1391.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Classification and Review of the Charging Strategies for Commercial Lithium-Ion Batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Gao, X. Zhang, Q. Cheng, B. Guo, J. Yang, Classification and Review of the Charging Strategies for Commercial Lithium-Ion Batteries, IEEE Access, 7 (2019) 43511-43524.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Märker, C. Xu, C.P. Grey, Operando NMR of NMC811/Graphite Lithium-Ion Batteries: Structure, Dynamics, and Lithium Metal Deposition, J. Am. Chem. Soc., 142 (2020) 17447-17456.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Germanium as negative electrode material for sodium-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Baggetto, J.K. Keum, J.F. Browning, G.M. Veith, Germanium as negative electrode material for sodium-ion batteries, Electrochem. commun., 34 (2013) 41-44.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Characterization of bi-material electrodes for electrochemical hybrid energy storage devices

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In situ Raman microscopy during discharge of a high capacity silicon–carbon composite Li-ion battery negative electrode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Nanda, M.K. Datta, J.T. Remillard, A. O’Neill, P.N. Kumta, In situ Raman microscopy during discharge of a high capacity silicon–carbon composite Li-ion battery negative electrode, Electrochem. commun., 11 (2009) 235-237.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.S. Zhang, Identifying rate limitation and a guide to design of fast-charging Li-ion battery, InfoMat, 2 (2020) 942-949.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Mao, R.E. Ruther, J. Li, Z. Du, I. Belharouak, Identifying the limiting electrode in lithium ion batteries for extreme fast charging, Electrochem. commun., 97 (2018) 37-41.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Atlung, K. West, T. Jacobsen, Dynamic Aspects of Solid Solution Cathodes for Electrochemical Power Sources, J. Electrochem. Soc., 126 (1979) 1311-1321.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lithium-Diffusion Induced Capacity Losses in Lithium-Based Batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Rehnlund, Z. Wang, L. Nyholm, Lithium-Diffusion Induced Capacity Losses in Lithium-Based Batteries, Adv. Mater., 34 (2022) 2108827.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Revealing the correlation between structural evolution and Li+ diffusion kinetics of nickel-rich cathode materials in Li-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Li, X.-Z. Yuan, L. Zhang, D. Song, K. Shi, C. Bock, Degradation Mechanisms and Mitigation Strategies of Nickel-Rich NMC-Based Lithium-Ion Batteries, Electrochem. Energ. Rev., 3 (2020) 43-80.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Jiang, D.L. Danilov, R.-A. Eichel, P.H.L. Notten, A Review of Degradation Mechanisms and Recent Achievements for Ni-Rich Cathode-Based Li-Ion Batteries, Adv. Energy Mater., 11 (2021) 2103005.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Core-shell Ni-rich NMC-Nanocarbon cathode from scalable solvent-free mechanofusion for high-performance 18650 Li-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Phattharasupakun, J. Wutthiprom, S. Duangdangchote, S. Sarawutanukul, C. Tomon, F. Duriyasart, S. Tubtimkuna, C. Aphirakaramwong, M. Sawangphruk, Core-shell Ni-rich NMC-Nanocarbon cathode from scalable solvent-free mechanofusion for high-performance 18650 Li-ion batteries, Energy Storage Mater., 36 (2021) 485-495.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: What Limits the Capacity of Layered Oxide Cathodes in Lithium Batteries?
